programs.name: South Dakota Medicaid for Seniors/Disabled

eligibility.income_limits: For 2026 Long-Term Care Medicaid (relevant for seniors/disabled): Single: $2,901/month; Married (both applying): $5,802/month; Married (one applying): $2,901/month for applicant (spousal impoverishment rules apply for community spouse). SSI recipients auto-qualify. General Medicaid for disabled: varies by category, e.g., up to 138% FPL (~$2,163/month for single in 2026 ACA context, but LTC has specific limits). Full table per household size not explicitly detailed; consult DSS for exact FPL-based adjustments.[1][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: Single: $2,000; Married (both applying): $4,000; Married (one applying): $2,000 for applicant ($148,620 Community Spouse Resource Allowance for non-applicant spouse in 2026). Counts: bank accounts, stocks, bonds, second vehicles, income-producing property. Exempt: primary home (up to $713,000 equity if intent to return), one vehicle, personal belongings, burial plots, life insurance (face value ≤$1,500), irrevocable burial trusts.[1][6]
other_requirements: South Dakota resident
U.S. citizen or qualified alien
Valid Social Security Number
Assign rights to medical support/payment
Nursing Home Level of Care (NHLOC) for LTC/Nursing Home/Waivers (medical need for long-term care)
Disabled as determined by SSA or DSS (for non-aged)
Functional need for ADLs for some regular Medicaid LTC services

benefits.value: Long-term care services including nursing home care, home health aide, personal attendant services, home modifications (if unable to live safely at home independently), hospital stays, doctor visits, prescriptions. Covers services Medicare does not, like certain home-based long-term supports. No fixed dollar amounts or hours specified; based on medical need and approved level of care.[1][4][7]

methods: Online: http://dss.sd.gov/applyonline
Phone: 1-855-256-6742 (eligibility calculator line; general DSS contact)
Mail: Any local DSS office
In-person: Local DSS office
application.processing_time: 45 days for most applications; 90 days if disability determination required.[5]
application.waitlist: Not mentioned in sources; may apply for specific waivers/services.[1]
forms: South Dakota Medicaid/CHIP Application (online or paper via DSS)
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, SSI awards)
Proof of assets (bank statements, property deeds)

geography.regional_variations: Processing at local DSS offices may have minor wait time differences; no specified provider or benefit variations by region.[5]
offices_or_providers: Local Department of Social Services (DSS) offices statewide

gotchas: SSI auto-eligibility, but LTC requires separate NHLOC determination.
Over limits? Spend-down or qualify via spousal impoverishment/Medicaid planning (e.g., exemptions, trusts).[1]
30-day resource assessment for disability; must meet or have income below SSI rate ($914 in 2023, adjusted).[2]
Must present Medicaid card for services or risk denial.[5]
Work requirement possible for expansion adults under 65 (pending federal approval).[3]
programs.data_shape_notes: LTC eligibility has strict NHLOC and separate income/asset tables by marital status; SSI auto-qualifies but waivers/nursing require medical review; spousal protections via Community Spouse Resource Allowance.

programs.name: HOPE Waiver (Home & Community-Based Options and Person Centered Excellence)

eligibility.age: 65 or older, OR 18-64 with a qualifying disability from Social Security Administration[1][2]

income_limits.description: Income limit is 300% of the Federal Benefit Rate (FBR), which adjusts annually in January[3]
income_limits.married_applicants: Each spouse evaluated individually; each allowed up to the monthly limit[1][3]
income_limits.spousal_income_treatment: For married couples where only one spouse applies, spousal income is not counted toward the applicant's limit[1]
income_limits.needs_allowance: Up to $3,160.50 per month of joint income can be allocated to the non-applicant spouse[1]

asset_limits.single_or_widowed: Up to $2,000 in countable assets (cash in checking/savings accounts and similar accessible resources)[1]
asset_limits.married_couples_both_applicants: Each spouse must meet the $2,000 limit individually[1]
asset_limits.married_one_applicant: Applicant must have $2,000 or less; non-applicant spouse may have up to $126,420 in countable assets[1]
asset_limits.home_equity_exemption: Home is exempt if applicant lives in it or has intent to return; home equity interest cannot exceed $730,000 (2025 limit)[3]
asset_limits.additional_exemptions: Home is also exempt if spouse, minor child (under 21), or disabled/blind child of any age lives in the home[3]

other_requirements: Must be a South Dakota resident[6]
Must meet nursing facility level of care (NFLOC) as determined by standardized assessment[2][4]
Cannot currently reside in a hospital, nursing facility, or ICF/MR (Intermediate Care Facility for Mentally Retarded) setting[1]
Must receive at least one waiver service at least once per month[1]
Must participate in initial and ongoing assessments[8]
Must have an individual support plan prepared[2]

benefits.value: Home and community-based services for individuals who need nursing facility level of care[5]
benefits.varies_by: Individual need and support plan; no specific dollar amounts or hour limits provided in search results
benefits.note: Services are individualized based on nursing facility level of care determination and support plan

methods: Phone: Dakota at Home (833) 663-9673[5]
Referral: Dakota at Home Referral for HOPE Waiver/State Plan Services[4]
application.waitlist: Program capped at 1,834 concurrent recipients as of 2019[1]; current waitlist status not specified in search results
forms: Home Care Assessment (HCA) tool used to determine nursing facility level of care[3]
documents: Call Dakota at Home for specific document requirements[5]
application_process_steps: LTSS Assessment completed by LTSS Case Management Specialist[4]
Department determines if participant meets nursing facility level of care[4]
SD DSS Long Term Care Benefits Specialist determines financial eligibility[4]

geography.restrictions: Must be a South Dakota resident[6]
geography.regional_variations: Not specified in search results; program appears to be administered statewide through Long Term Service and Supports (LTSS)[5]
geography.offices_or_providers: Contact Dakota at Home (833) 663-9673 for provider and office locations[5]

programs.official_source: South Dakota Department of Social Services (dss.sd.gov); Medicaid.gov waiver listing: medicaid.gov/medicaid/section-1115-demo/demonstration-and-waiver-list/83171[7]
gotchas: Program has a hard cap on concurrent recipients (1,834 as of 2019)[1] — families should verify current availability before investing time in application
Applicant cannot currently live in a nursing facility, hospital, or ICF/MR setting to qualify[1] — this is a community-based program only
Income and asset limits are strictly enforced; applying when over limits will result in automatic denial[3]
Home equity limit of $730,000 (2025) applies even if home is exempt[3] — high-value homes may disqualify applicants
Must receive at least one waiver service monthly to maintain eligibility[1] — passive enrollment without service use is not permitted
Nursing facility level of care determination is required[2] — not all elderly or disabled individuals qualify; functional need must be demonstrated
Income limits are 300% of Federal Benefit Rate, which adjusts annually[3] — families should verify current year limits before applying
Waiver approval expires September 30, 2026[7] — renewal status should be verified as this date approaches
Specific dollar amounts for services and processing timelines are not publicly detailed[4] — families must contact Dakota at Home for detailed benefit information
programs.data_shape_notes: This program's eligibility is highly individualized based on functional need (nursing facility level of care) rather than categorical eligibility. Income and asset limits are fixed statewide but adjusted annually. The program operates under a concurrent recipient cap, creating potential waitlist dynamics. Benefits are service-based and individualized through support plans rather than fixed dollar amounts. The program is administered statewide through LTSS but specific regional variations in processing time, provider availability, or service delivery are not documented in available sources.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Before you apply:** Verify your loved one meets the **nursing facility level of care** requirement—this is the primary gate, not just age or disability status[2][4]. The program is capped at 1,834 concurrent recipients[1], so availability may be limited.

**Income and assets matter:** The 2025 income limit is $2,901/month for single applicants[3], and countable assets are capped at $2,000[1]. However, the home is protected if your loved one lives in it or intends to return, up to $730,000 in equity[3].

**Start by calling:** Contact Dakota at Home at (833) 663-9673[5]—this is your entry point for referral, assessment scheduling, and specific questions about current availability and processing times.

**This is community-based only:** Your loved one must live at home or in a community setting; they cannot currently be in a nursing facility, hospital, or ICF/MR to qualify[1].

**Services are individualized:** Rather than a fixed benefit amount, the program provides services based on an individual support plan[2]—in-home care, respite, meals, adult day services, and assisted living options[5].

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to anyone 55+ regardless of income, though most participants are dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid[1][2][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned; eligibility not based on assets[1]
other_requirements: Live in a PACE service area
Certified by the state as needing nursing home level of care
Able to live safely in the community with PACE support[1][3][4][8]

benefits.value: Comprehensive medical and social services via interdisciplinary team (primary care doctor, nurse, therapists, social worker, dietician, etc.), including adult day care, meals, home care, transportation, lab/X-ray, dentistry, hospital/nursing home care when needed, nutritional counseling, preventive care, home modifications, equipment; all coordinated to allow community living instead of nursing home[1][3][5]

application.processing_time: Initial assessment within 30 days of enrollment; care plan semi-annually thereafter[7]

geography.restrictions: Only available in areas with PACE providers; requires living near a PACE center
geography.regional_variations: Program not yet implemented in South Dakota; feasibility studies conducted but no active providers or service areas[7][8][10]

programs.official_source: https://dhs.sd.gov (South Dakota DHS; no specific PACE page as program not active); national: https://www.cms.gov/medicare/medicaid-coordination/about/pace[2]
gotchas: PACE is not currently available in South Dakota; only in states with approved providers (SD has feasibility studies but no operational programs)[7][8][10]
Must live in a designated PACE service area near a center; no statewide coverage[1][3]
Becomes sole source of Medicare/Medicaid services for dually eligible enrollees; voluntary and can disenroll anytime[4]
95% of participants live in community, but covers nursing home if needed[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: Not available in South Dakota (feasibility stage only, no providers or service areas); nationally limited to specific PACE centers/service areas, no income/asset tests

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

eligibility.income_limits: South Dakota follows federal guidelines with state-specific limits that vary by program and year. For 2026 (current year), federal limits are: QMB - Individual: $1,350/month, Couple: $1,824/month; SLMB and QI have higher limits (e.g., individual ~$1,526-$1,715 based on prior year data, adjusted annually to ~120-135% FPL). 2025 SD-specific examples: QI individual <$1,781/month, couple <$2,400/month (includes $20 disregard). Limits based on countable income after disregards; full household table not specified beyond individual/couple—contact DSS for exact current table by size[2][4][6].
eligibility.asset_limits: Applies to all programs: Individual $9,430-$9,950 (varies by source/year), Couple $14,130-$14,910. Counts: checking/savings, CDs. Exempt: primary home, one car, burial plots, irrevocable burial trusts. Older SD source notes lower $4,000 individual/$6,000 couple—use current federal-aligned limits and verify with state[1][3][4][6].
other_requirements: Must be entitled to/receiving Medicare Part A and/or B (both A&B for SLMB/QI).
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant.
Reside in South Dakota.
For QI: Annual reapplication required; first-come, first-served with priority to prior recipients.

benefits.value: QMB: Pays Medicare Part A/B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, copays. SLMB/QI: Pays Part B premiums only. Automatic qualification for Extra Help (Part D low-income subsidy, e.g., ≤$12.65 copay per drug in 2026). No specific dollar amounts beyond premiums (Part B ~$185/month in 2026, state pays directly to Medicare).[2][4]

methods: Mail/PDF form: Download 'Application for Medicare Savings Programs' from https://dss.sd.gov/formsandpubs/docs/MEDELGBLTY/270Medicaresavingsapplication.pdf[2].
Phone: Call DSS at 1-877-999-5612 (toll-free) or TTY/TDD 1-877-486-2048; ask for benefits specialist[6].
In-person/mail: Local DSS office or SHIINE volunteer assistance (free counseling/application help)[6].
Online info: https://dss.sd.gov/medicaid/Eligibility/default.aspx[2]. No fully online application specified.
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; typically 45 days for Medicaid-related apps—verify with DSS.
application.waitlist: QI has first-come, first-served with waitlist possible if funding exhausted (priority to prior year recipients)[4].
forms: Application for Medicare Savings Programs (form implied as 270 in URL)[2]

geography.regional_variations: Administered uniformly by state DSS; local SHIINE volunteers available variably by area for assistance—no noted differences in eligibility/benefits/wait times[6].
offices_or_providers: Statewide DSS offices; local SHIINE counselors

gotchas: Income/resource limits change annually (e.g., 2025 vs 2026)—always verify current with DSS as sources conflict slightly[1][4][6].
QI requires annual reapplication and has funding caps/waitlists[4].
Countable income uses disregards ($20 general, others)—gross income may overestimate ineligibility[6].
Automatic Extra Help enrollment often missed[1][4].
Exempt assets like burial trusts overlooked[3][6].
Must have Medicare entitlement; not for non-Medicare eligible.
programs.data_shape_notes: Tiered by program (QMB fullest benefits, SLMB/QI premiums only); income scales to FPL % (100%/120%/135%); assets individual/couple only (no full household table); QI funding-limited with priority tiers.

programs.name: SNAP/Food Assistance (Senior Box Program/CSFP)

eligibility.income_limits: Household income at or below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Exact dollar amounts vary annually; contact local agency for current table as specific figures not listed in sources for South Dakota (federal guidelines published by HHS).[3][6][7]
other_requirements: South Dakota resident.
Must reside in a participating service area (local agencies may designate).

benefits.value: Monthly box of non-perishable USDA foods at no cost, including cheese, juice, oats, ready-to-eat cereal, rice, pasta, peanut butter, dry beans, canned meat, poultry, or fish, canned fruits and vegetables, and often a canned entree such as beef stew or chili.

methods: Call local agency or Feeding South Dakota (example: 605-853-3656 for Miller site).
Contact state or local distribution sites for in-person application.
No specific online URL or mail address listed; apply via phone or in-person at local agencies.
application.waitlist: Not mentioned; may vary by local availability.
documents: Proof or knowledge of household gross monthly income.

geography.restrictions: Available through local public or non-profit agencies; must reside in designated service area.
geography.regional_variations: Distributed by local agencies with varying sites (e.g., Feeding South Dakota in Miller at 610 E. 4th Avenue; Watertown at 621 4th Street SE); call for distribution times and local providers.
offices_or_providers: Feeding South Dakota ( statewide service area, example Miller: 605-853-3656).
Local agencies via SD Department of Education.

gotchas: Separate from SNAP; can receive both simultaneously.
No automatic dual eligibility mentioned for SSI (unlike some states).
Availability depends on local agency stock and service areas; call ahead.
All participants must be 60+; no women/infants/children since 2014.
Income is gross monthly before taxes at 150% FPL specific to SD (higher than federal max of 130%).
programs.data_shape_notes: State-administered via local agencies; income at 150% FPL; fixed monthly food box; no asset test; distribution sites vary by region.

programs.name: Low Income Energy Assistance Program (LIEAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Gross monthly household income must be at or below 200% of the federal poverty level, based on the most recent three months of income for all household members. Households where all members receive SNAP are automatically categorically eligible. Specific annual income limits for larger households (from DSS table): 7-person: $22,514; 8-person: $23,014; 9-person: $23,514; 10-person: $24,431. Limits scale by household size but exact table for 1-6 not detailed in sources; check official DSS for current full table.[5][2]
other_requirements: Household must be responsible for heating costs (paid directly to supplier or included in rent).
Application must be signed by all household members age 18+.
Funding availability affects eligibility.[3]

benefits.value: One-time supplemental payment for home heating costs, paid directly to utility or energy supplier. Exact dollar amounts vary by household income, size, fuel type, and funding; not fixed per source. Related: Cooling Assistance $1025 for eligible LIEAP recipients; ECIP for crises; Weatherization for efficiency upgrades (no fixed $).[1][2][5]

methods: Online: https://www.sd.gov/cs?id=sc_cat_item&sys_id=a254bd6edbf7f410b2fb93d4f3961974 (Low Income Energy Assistance Application).[6]
Mail: South Dakota Department of Social Services, Office of Energy Assistance, 700 Governors Drive, Pierre, SD 57501.[5]
Email: Scan and send to DSSHeat@state.sd.us.[5]
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; apply early as funding is limited and may close when depleted.[1]
application.waitlist: Possible for Weatherization due to limited funds; not specified for main LIEAP.[5]
forms: Low Income Energy Assistance Application (online via SD.gov).
Vendor Claim Form (online for energy suppliers).[5]
documents: Proof of household income for the 3 calendar months prior to application month (for everyone in household).
Proof of home heating and electric costs (bills paid to supplier or as part of rent).[5]

geography.restrictions: None statewide, but Weatherization completed by four community action programs serving specific counties.[5]
geography.regional_variations: Weatherization varies by county (contact local community action program); potential differences in processing or wait times by local agency/funding; heating assistance statewide via central DSS office.[1][5]
offices_or_providers: Central: DSS Office of Energy Assistance, 700 Governors Drive, Pierre, SD 57501.
Weatherization: Four community action programs by county (list available on DSS site).[5]

gotchas: Funding limited; apply early in fall/winter as applications may close when funds deplete (not year-round).[1]
No cooling assistance from main LIEAP; separate Cooling program only for prior/recent LIEAP recipients paying own electric.[2]
Crisis (ECIP) requires active LIEAP case plus emergency like shutoff.[2][5]
Roommates sharing utility bill count as one household.[1]
Weatherization needs landlord permission for renters + landlord contribution; priority to elderly/disabled/families.[5]
programs.data_shape_notes: Income at 200% FPL with 3-month lookback; no asset test; benefits as one-time payment varying by income/size/fuel; Weatherization county-specific via 4 providers; categorical eligibility via SNAP/active LIEAP for related programs.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance

eligibility.income_limits: Annual household income up to 200% of the federal poverty level. Households currently eligible for LIEAP (Low Income Energy Assistance Program) are categorically income eligible. Exact limits vary by household size and year; for reference, LIEAP-related table shows higher household sizes (e.g., 7-person: $22,514; 8-person: $23,014; 9-person: $23,514; 10-person: $24,431), but WAP uses 200% FPL directly[1][2][3].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in program guidelines[1][2].
other_requirements: Priority given to elderly, individuals with disabilities, families with children under 19, and single-family dwellings[1][2][6].
Home must not have received weatherization services in the previous 15 years[5].
Renters eligible only with written landlord permission; landlord contributes 1/3 of costs (or full if income below 200% FPL in some cases)[2][5].
Proof of ownership required for owner-occupied (deed or property tax notice)[4][5].
Home must pass energy audit and be feasible for rehabilitation[3][5].

benefits.value: Free home weatherization services including: weather-stripping doors/windows, caulking/sealing cracks, insulating attics/walls/under floors, repairing/tuning/replacing non-functional heating systems, incidental repairs to protect materials. May include air sealing, appliance repair/replacement, window/door/furnace replacement based on energy audit. Average annual savings ~$372 per household[1][2][3][4]. No direct dollar amount per home; one-time grant-like service.

methods: Contact Community Action Agency serving your county (county-specific; see map in dss.sd.gov/formsandpubs/docs/ENERGY/weatherizationbro.pdf or list on dss.sd.gov/economicassistance/energy_weatherization_assistance.aspx)[1][2].
Download printable application from dss.sd.gov/weatherization or dss.sd.gov/formsandpubs/docs/ENERGY/WeatherizationApplication.pdf[1][6].

Examples: WSDCA (Western SD) - call 605-348-1460, email kpalmer@wsdca.org, or online form at wsdca.org/weatherization-assistance-application[4]; GROW SD (Sisseton area) - mail to 104 Ash Street East, Sisseton, SD 57262 or email info@growsd.org, phone 605-698-7654[5]; Inter-Lakes CAP (Madison area) - contact Carolyn Feige at 111 N Van Eps Ave, Madison SD 57042[7].
application.processing_time: Not specified; energy audit conducted post-eligibility[3].
application.waitlist: Funds limited; may be placed on waiting list[2].
forms: South Dakota Weatherization Assistance Application (printable PDF at dss.sd.gov/formsandpubs/docs/ENERGY/WeatherizationApplication.pdf or agency-specific like GROW SD version)[5][6].
documents: Proof of all household income for last 3 months prior to application (wage stubs, checks, employer statements, tax returns for self-employed)[2][5][6].
Proof of home ownership (deed, property tax notice) or landlord permission for renters[4][5].
Proof of heating/electric costs if applicable[2].
LIEAP CID if receiving Energy Assistance (simplifies process)[6].

geography.restrictions: Services via 4 community action programs by county; must contact agency for your county[2].
geography.regional_variations: Administered by different community action agencies (e.g., WSDCA western counties, GROW SD northeast, Inter-Lakes CAP southeast); contact info, forms, and submission methods vary by agency; potential differences in wait times due to limited funds[1][2][4][5][7].
offices_or_providers: Community Action Agencies by county (full list/map at dss.sd.gov/economicassistance/energy_weatherization_assistance.aspx and dss.sd.gov/formsandpubs/docs/ENERGY/weatherizationbro.pdf)[1][2].
Examples: WSDCA, GROW South Dakota, Inter-Lakes Community Action Partnership[4][5][7].

gotchas: Must contact specific county Community Action Agency; not centralized DSS intake[1][2].
Renters need landlord permission and contribution (1/3 cost typically)[2][5].
Home ineligible if weatherized in last 15 years[5].
LIEAP participants are auto-eligible (income-wise), speeding process[3][6].
Waiting lists common due to limited funds[2].
Application denied if incomplete or missing proof[6].
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered regionally by 4 county-specific community action agencies; priority tiers (elderly/disabled/children); renters require landlord buy-in; ties to LIEAP for categorical eligibility; no asset test, income at 200% FPL.

programs.name: Senior Health Information and Insurance Education (SHIINE)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to all Medicare beneficiaries, those approaching Medicare, and their caregivers[3][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or tests apply[3][4]
other_requirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary in South Dakota, approaching Medicare eligibility, or a caregiver of someone on/approaching Medicare[3][4]

benefits.value: Free, confidential, unbiased one-on-one counseling on Medicare Parts A/B, Part D prescription drugs, Medicare Advantage (HMOs/PPOs), Medigap, long-term care insurance, Medicare Savings Programs (QMB/SLMB/QI), prescription assistance, Medicaid, other insurance/free health programs; assistance with Low Income Subsidy (LIS) enrollment, plan comparison to avoid overpaying; fraud prevention via Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP); educational outreach events, printed materials, referrals[1][5]

methods: Website: https://dhs.sd.gov/en/ltss/shiine or https://www.shiine.net[2][3]
Email: caitlin.christensen@state.sd.us or region-specific like western_region@shiine.net[2][4]
In-person or phone appointment scheduling via regional coordinators or trained volunteer counselors statewide[4][5][6]
application.processing_time: Immediate counseling appointment scheduling; no formal processing as it's not an entitlement program[4]
forms: No specific forms required; verbal/phone appointment suffices[4]

geography.restrictions: None; covers all of South Dakota[3][4]
geography.regional_variations: Three regional coordinators oversee volunteers and events; minimum two outreach events per year at each Native American reservation per region; contact regional coordinators for local services[1][6]
offices_or_providers: South Dakota Department of Social Services, 700 Governors Drive, Pierre SD 57501-2291 (state office); regional coordinators in three regions (Western/Central, others unspecified in sources)[2][4][6]

gotchas: Not a healthcare or financial aid program—provides education/counseling only, no direct payments or medical services; volunteers cannot have active insurance licenses; not affiliated with insurance sellers[1][3][6]
During Medicare Open Enrollment (e.g., Oct 15-Dec 7 for 2026 changes), expect higher demand for Part D/LIS help[1][6]
Electronic data transmission must be encrypted for privacy[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: No income/asset/age tests—universal access for Medicare-related population; regionally coordinated volunteer network with required outreach to Native reservations; counseling-focused SHIP program, not benefits-paying

programs.name: Meals on Wheels

eligibility.income_limits: No strict statewide income limits; programs prioritize low-income seniors and follow federal poverty guidelines in some areas (e.g., commodity meals for households at or below 150% of federal poverty level). Medicaid/SSI recipients often qualify. Suggested donations requested but no one denied for inability to pay[1][3][6].
eligibility.asset_limits: Not applicable; no asset tests mentioned across programs[1][3].
other_requirements: Homebound and unable to cook or shop independently[1][2][6][8][9]
Spouse or dependent adult of 60+ participant may qualify regardless of age[2][3]
Under 60 must contact Dakota at Home for separate eligibility assessment[2][8]

benefits.value: One hot nutritious home-delivered meal per weekday (Monday-Friday); congregate dining also available at sites. Suggested donation $4.25-$5.25/meal (under 60 pay full ~$6.50); private pay option $15/meal in some areas. Funded by Older Americans Act[3][6][8][9].

methods: Call local provider (e.g., Active Generations: (605) 333-3305 or 1-833-663-9673; Dakota at Home for under 60[2][8]
Enroll online via Dakota at Home Referral Form[2][8]
Request brochure by mail from Meals on Wheels Western SD[3]
Contact local senior center or Meals on Wheels provider directly[1]
application.processing_time: Not specified; immediate enrollment encouraged where available[2][3].
application.waitlist: Limited meals available; priority for need but no explicit waitlists noted[3].
forms: Dakota at Home Referral Form (for under 60 or general inquiries)[2][8]
documents: Proof of age (e.g., driver's license, birth certificate)
Doctor’s note or medical records if disabled/under 60
Proof of income if requested (not always required)

geography.restrictions: Administered locally through senior centers and non-profits; coverage varies by community (e.g., western SD: 64 communities; Sioux Falls, Tabor, Active Generations areas)[2][3][6][8]
geography.regional_variations: Different providers (e.g., Meals on Wheels Western SD, Active Generations, The Center in Tabor, Valley Senior Services); suggested donations vary ($4.25-$5.25); some offer congregate dining or commodity foods[3][6][8][9].
offices_or_providers: Meals on Wheels Western South Dakota[3]
Meals on Wheels Sioux Falls - W. 46th Street[2]
Active Generations (605) 333-3305[8]
The Center, Tabor SD[6]

programs.official_source: https://dakotaathome.sd.gov/ (Dakota at Home portal for referrals and local programs)
gotchas: Not a single statewide program—must contact local provider; homebound status key, not just age[1][9]
Suggested donations appreciated but no denial for non-payment; limited meals may prioritize lowest need[3]
Under 60 requires separate Dakota at Home check, not standard Meals on Wheels[2][8]
Varies by exact community; some areas have private pay only if slots full[3][5]
programs.data_shape_notes: Decentralized by region with local non-profits; no uniform income/asset test, emphasis on homebound 60+; Older Americans Act funded with donations; under 60 routed to Dakota at Home

programs.name: Respite Care/Caregiver Support (HOPE Waiver & Family Support 360)

eligibility.age: 65 or older, or 18-64 with qualifying disability (SSA designation or needs requiring long-term supports/services)
eligibility.income_limits: Applicant income up to 300% of Federal Benefit Rate (FBR): $2,313/month (2019)[1], $2,901/month (2025)[2], regardless of marital status; each spouse considered individually if both applying; spousal Needs Allowance up to $3,160.50/month joint income[1]; no household size table provided
eligibility.asset_limits: $2,000 countable assets for single/widowed applicants (cash, checking/savings, accessible resources); married: applicant $2,000, non-applicant spouse up to $126,420[1]; home equity ≤$730,000 if intent to return (2025), exempt if spouse/minor/disabled child in home[2]
other_requirements: South Dakota resident
Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) via Home Care Assessment (HCA) tool
Not resident of hospital, nursing facility, or ICF/MR
Receive at least one waiver service monthly
Needs assessment and LTSS assessment by Case Management Specialist

benefits.value: Home and community-based services including respite and caregiver services, in-home services, nutrition/meals, safety/transportation, adult day services, community living home, assisted living; specific to respite/caregiver support as core services[5]

methods: Phone: Dakota at Home (833) 663-9673[5]
Dakota At Home Referral for HOPE Waiver/State Plan Services
application.waitlist: Capped at 1,834 concurrent recipients (2019 data); implies waitlist when full[1]
forms: Dakota At Home Referral for HOPE Waiver/State Plan Services
documents: Call for information[5]; prepare income/asset proof, disability documentation, needs assessment materials

geography.regional_variations: LTSS provider enrollment varies by service; regional offices/providers via Dakota at Home (e.g., Pierre office)[3][5]
offices_or_providers: Dakota at Home statewide; example: Pierre - E. Highway 34[5]

programs.official_source: https://dss.sd.gov (DSS Long Term Services and Supports); Medicaid.gov waiver page: https://www.medicaid.gov/medicaid/section-1115-demo/demonstration-and-waiver-list/83171[7]
gotchas: Strict cap on slots (1,834 in 2019) leads to waitlists[1]
Must use at least one service monthly or risk losing eligibility[1]
Income/asset limits updated annually (FBR-based); apply only if qualified to avoid denial[2]
Home equity limit applies unless exemptions met[2]
Family Support 360 not detailed in sources; may be separate or branded caregiver program layered on HOPE
Provider must have DHS/LTSS contract and Medicaid enrollment[3]
programs.data_shape_notes: Waiver capped by slots, not household size; NFLOC via specific HCA tool; respite/caregiver core but part of broad HCBS; annual FBR income updates; provider enrollment service-specific

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

eligibility.income_limits: Annual family income not more than 125% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL). Exact dollar amounts vary annually and by household size; for reference, North Dakota's 2023 figures (similar guidelines) were: 1: $19,563; 2: $24,643; 3: $33,313; 4: $40,188. Check current FPL via HHS for South Dakota[1][2][6].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in South Dakota-specific sources.
other_requirements: Resident of South Dakota
Unemployed
Eligible to work in the United States

benefits.value: Part-time community service work (average 20 hours/week) at minimum wage (highest of federal, state, or local); training and counseling; job placement assistance; free annual physical exams; support for independence and social activities[1][2][3].

methods: Electronic: SCSEP application form (Adobe PDF) via DLR website
In-person or pickup: Any South Dakota Department of Labor and Regulation (DLR) local office
Contact workforce experts at DLR local offices for assistance (no specific statewide phone listed; use local office contacts or general DLR inquiry)
application.waitlist: Not specified; may vary by region due to limited coverage areas
documents: Proof of age, residency, income, unemployment status, and work eligibility (e.g., ID, pay stubs, tax returns; specifics not detailed—prepare standard verification documents)

geography.restrictions: Offered only in three areas: West River, East River, and Central (see coverage map on DLR site for details)
geography.regional_variations: Administered in three distinct regions (West River, East River, Central) with region-specific contacts; potential differences in wait times, availability, or host sites by area[2].
offices_or_providers: South Dakota Department of Labor and Regulation (DLR) local offices in West River, East River, and Central areas (map with contacts on DLR SCSEP page)

gotchas: Not statewide—limited to West River, East River, Central areas only; check map first

Priority enrollment for veterans/qualified spouses, then those 65+, disabled, rural residents, homeless/at-risk, low literacy/English proficiency, low employment prospects, or prior American Job Center users[3]
Income is family-based at 125% FPL (updates yearly; verify current levels)
Must be unemployed and seeking workforce transition; not permanent employment
No asset test, but strict unemployment and residency rules
programs.data_shape_notes: Limited to 3 regional areas (not statewide); income at 125% FPL scales by household size; priority tiers affect access; no fixed processing times or waitlist info available

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors

eligibility.income_limits: No strict income limit for seniors; individuals age 60+ are eligible regardless of income at Dakota Plains Legal Services (DPLS) and some East River Legal Services (ERLS) programs. For general low-income eligibility (if under 60), typically 125-200% of Federal Poverty Guidelines depending on provider and priority (e.g., ERLS: 125% standard, up to 200% in cases; Access to Justice: 200% for elderly). Exact FPG amounts vary annually by household size and are calculated at application; no fixed table provided in sources.
eligibility.asset_limits: Non-exempt assets may be considered at ERLS for some services (details unspecified); no asset limits mentioned for seniors specifically at DPLS or statewide senior programs.
other_requirements: South Dakota resident in service area (varies by provider)
Greatest economic and social need for DHS-funded vulnerable older adults program
U.S. citizen or permanent legal resident (ERLS)
Case must fit program priorities (e.g., elder abuse, long-term care, consumer issues)

benefits.value: Civil legal assistance including advice only, brief services (investigation, paperwork preparation), or full representation as counsel of record. Focus for seniors: elder abuse, long-term care, guardianship/conservatorship. No financial payouts; limited by resources and case merits.

methods: Online: https://www.sdlawhelp.org/apply (serves DPLS, ERLS, Access to Justice)
Phone: DPLS local offices (check Areas Served page), ERLS (605) 336-9230 or (800) 952-3015, Access to Justice 855-287-3510, Disaster Hotline (605) 444-3719
Email: access.to.justice@sdbar.net
In-person: Local offices (e.g., DPLS, ERLS in eastern SD counties)
application.processing_time: Not specified; intake screening first, then attorney referral if eligible.
application.waitlist: Subject to staff/volunteer availability and program resources; no formal waitlist details.
forms: Online intake application via sdlawhelp.org/apply; complete application required prior to assistance (ERLS)
documents: Proof of income (e.g., pay stubs, tax returns)

geography.restrictions: Varies by provider: DPLS (western/central SD, check Areas Served), ERLS (33 eastern counties: Aurora, Beadle, Bon Homme, etc.), Access to Justice (statewide referral)
geography.regional_variations: Different providers by region (DPLS east/west divide); eligibility exceptions and services limited by local resources/staff.
offices_or_providers: Dakota Plains Legal Services (DPLS)
East River Legal Services (ERLS)
Access to Justice (A2J)
DHS Legal Services for Vulnerable Older Adults

gotchas: Not all eligible cases accepted due to federal case priority rules and resource limits
Seniors over 60 bypass income test at DPLS but still need qualifying case type
Preparation of paperwork does not commit to court representation
Must reside in provider's service counties; referrals given if outside area
Apply even if unsure—eligibility determined during screening
programs.data_shape_notes: Multiple regional providers with varying income thresholds (seniors often exempt); unified online application at sdlawhelp.org; services tiered by case priority and resources, not fixed hours/dollars

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to all regardless of financial status.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; no financial tests apply.
other_requirements: Residing in or advocating for someone in a nursing home, assisted living community, or community living home in South Dakota[4][5].

benefits.value: Information and referral assistance; education on residents' rights; complaint investigation and resolution; conflict mediation; representation to ensure fair treatment, health, safety, welfare, and rights in long-term care facilities. No financial aid, hours, or dollar amounts provided[5][7][9].

methods: Email: LTCO@state.sd.us (include 'Volunteer' in subject for volunteer roles, but services available directly)[8]
No formal application process or specific forms required; contact for assistance[4].
application.processing_time: Immediate assistance upon contact; no formal processing.

geography.restrictions: Limited to residents of long-term care facilities (nursing homes, assisted living, community living homes) anywhere in South Dakota[4][5].
geography.regional_variations: Statewide program with local ombudsmen (LLTCOs) as full-time state employees covering the state; extensive travel by staff ensures uniform access, no noted differences in services, wait times, or providers by region[1][5].
offices_or_providers: State office in Pierre; local ombudsmen statewide via Department of Human Services[1][5].

gotchas: Not a Medicaid or financial eligibility program—focuses solely on advocacy and rights protection, not services like healthcare or funding[2][5][7].
Available to family advocates, not just residents[4].
No application barriers, but services target long-term care facility residents only[4][5].
Often confused with long-term care funding programs[2].
programs.data_shape_notes: no income test; no formal application or documents; advocacy-only for long-term care facility residents statewide; volunteer certification option separate from receiving services

programs.name: Senior Companions of South Dakota

eligibility.age: 21 or older (primarily older adults; no upper limit specified for clients)
eligibility.income_limits: No income limits for clients receiving services. Income guidelines apply only to volunteers (age 55+): One person household: $30,120; Two person: $40,380; Three person: $49,720; Four person: $60,000; Add $5,140 per additional member. 50% of limits deductible for medical expenses.[2]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned for clients or volunteers.
other_requirements: Need-based: disabilities, lack of other assistance, living alone, recuperating from hospital/nursing home, terminally ill, Alzheimer's or related disorders, or caregiver respite needs.[1]
Services provided in homes or apartments.[1]

benefits.value: Free companionship and assistance including: regular visits/friendship, help with daily activities, light housekeeping, meal planning/preparation, playing games, reminiscing, transportation (for errands, medical appointments, grocery shopping), respite for caregivers.[1][3][7] Volunteers serve 10-40 hours/week (average 10-15), typically 2-4 clients per volunteer.[3][4]

methods: Contact referring agencies (e.g., Active Generations Sioux Falls (605) 336-6748; Avera@Home Pierre (605) 280-8768; others listed).[1]
In-person at regional offices or agencies.[1]
application.waitlist: Not mentioned; availability considered in eligibility.[1]
documents: No documents specified; contact office/agency for needs-based assessment.[1]

geography.restrictions: Primarily rural communities; available in homes/apartments statewide.[3]
geography.regional_variations: Regional offices: Sioux Falls and Rapid City with separate phone lines; referring agencies in Sioux Falls, Pierre, Brookings, Canistota.[1][2]
offices_or_providers: Sioux Falls: 1000 N West Avenue Suite 260, Sioux Falls, SD 57104 (implied); Rapid City: 2040 W Main St Ste 213, Rapid City, SD 57702; Various agencies.[1][6][7]

gotchas: Program matches low-income volunteers (55+) with clients; families apply to receive services, not become volunteers. No fees for clients, but volunteer availability determines service. Not professional healthcare—focuses on companionship/light assistance. Transportation limited to specific purposes (errands, medical, groceries).[1][2][3][7]
Eligibility for clients is needs-based, not income-tested—people miss that volunteers have strict income limits.[1][2]
programs.data_shape_notes: Two-sided program: free client services funded by stipended low-income volunteers (55+); no client income test, volunteer income scales by household size with medical deductions; regional phone lines and agency referrals.

programs.name: 60's Plus Dining

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits. Open to any resident aged 60 or older, spouses, and caregivers regardless of income.[1][2]
other_requirements: South Dakota resident.
For home-delivered meals: homebound due to illness or frailty.[1][2][4]

benefits.value: Nutritious meals via: Congregate meals (eaten at sites for socialization), Star Card (pre-paid card for meals at sites like breakfast/lunch/supper), Home Delivered (hot meals Monday-Friday for homebound), Frozen Meals (7-pack with 7 meals, bread, milk, juice). Suggested donation $5 per meal or $35 per frozen pack; no one turned away. No meal limits.[1][2][4]

methods: Phone: 605-256-6518 Ext 107 (Program Director Stacie Santema).[2]
In-person: Sites in 10 counties or ICAP office at 111 N Van Eps Ave, Madison SD 57042.[1][2]
Contact via https://sixtiesplusdining.com/contact/ for locations.[1]

geography.restrictions: Limited to 10 counties in east-central South Dakota: Brookings, Codington, Clark, Deuel, Grant, Hamlin, Kingsbury, Lake, Miner, Moody.[1][2]
geography.regional_variations: Services available at multiple congregate sites, Star Card sites, and home delivery within the 10 counties; specific sites vary by location (e.g., Watertown, Milbank, Madison).[1][2][4][6]
offices_or_providers: Inter-Lakes Community Action Partnership (ICAP), 111 N Van Eps Ave, Madison SD 57042.[2]
Various sites in 10 counties via sixtiesplusdining.com.[1]

gotchas: Not statewide—only 10 east-central counties; confuse with statewide programs like Senior Food Box (income-tested commodities).[1][2][3][5]
Donation-based but no one denied; some assume income test required.[1][2]
Spouses/caregivers eligible even if under 60.[1]
Home delivery requires being homebound.[2][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: Regional to 10 counties only; no income/asset test; multiple meal delivery options (congregate, prepaid card, home-delivered, frozen); donation-suggested, unlimited meals.

programs.name: Senior Box Program (CSFP)

eligibility.income_limits: Household income at or below 130-150% of federal poverty guidelines (FPL), varying by provider and year; states set limits at or below 130% FPL per USDA rules, but South Dakota providers cite 150% FPL. Exact dollar amounts published annually by HHS; e.g., for 2026, check current FPL tables as they adjust yearly—no household size table specified in sources, applies to household income.
other_requirements: Must reside in a participating South Dakota county or service area served by local providers (e.g., Minnehaha/Lincoln Counties for Salvation Army Sioux Falls; statewide via Feeding South Dakota in some areas).

benefits.value: Monthly box of USDA commodity foods (~40 lbs), including cereals, canned meat/fruits/vegetables, instant milk, peanut butter, instant potatoes, pasta, rice, oats, dry beans, juice, cheese; menus change monthly; provided at no cost for 6-12 months.

methods: Call local provider (e.g., Salvation Army Sioux Falls: 605-332-2331; Feeding South Dakota: 605-225-7410; Salvation Army Watertown: 605-886-4030)
Apply in-person during distribution hours (e.g., 2nd Thursday 10am-3pm at Sioux Falls; 4th Friday 9am-12pm at Watertown)
No statewide online or mail specified—contact local agency.
application.waitlist: Possible due to local capacity; varies by provider and county—not explicitly detailed.
forms: No specific form name/number listed; application via phone or in-person with local agency.
documents: Proof of age (e.g., ID), proof of income, proof of residency in service area.

geography.restrictions: Limited to counties served by local providers (e.g., Minnehaha/Lincoln for Sioux Falls Salvation Army; Clark/Codington/Deuel/Grant/Hamlin for Watertown Salvation Army; Brookings County via United Way; broader via Feeding South Dakota).
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local agencies (Salvation Army, Feeding South Dakota); income threshold cited as 130% or 150% FPL; distribution days/times vary (e.g., 2nd Thursday Sioux Falls, 4th Friday Watertown); delivery for homebound in some areas.
offices_or_providers: Salvation Army Sioux Falls (900 N. Cliff Ave, Sioux Falls; Minnehaha/Lincoln Counties)
Feeding South Dakota (1003 6th Ave SW, Aberdeen; broader SD)
Salvation Army Watertown (621 4th St SE, Watertown; Clark/Codington/Deuel/Grant/Hamlin Counties)
Brookings Area United Way (Brookings County)

gotchas: Not statewide—must check county/provider availability; cannot participate in both CSFP and WIC; income limits vary slightly by provider (130% vs 150% FPL); home delivery only for homebound; can combine with SNAP.
Eligibility strictly 60+ (no children/women/infants post-2014).
programs.data_shape_notes: County-restricted with local providers; income test at 130-150% FPL; no asset limits specified; distribution schedules and exact service areas vary by region.
